old_claim.note: Civic Contract Registries provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Postal money orders performed bank-like remittance functions at post offices, so mature banking and funds-transfer practice are the closest financial-service context.

source_shape.source_locator: RePEc abstract states that the postal money order system performed a major function of a bank by transferring funds cheaply, safely, and reliably to places with post offices.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source frames U.S. postal money orders as a nineteenth-century postal banking/remittance system.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A bank-like remittance source supports routing this payment product through banking and funds-transfer practice rather than through unrelated civic contract registries.

ontology_before: Postal Money Orders were modeled as a Classical-era courier-backed transfer dependent on civic contract registries.
ontology_after: Postal Money Orders are modeled as an Industrial-era postal financial product that depends on postal networks, currency value, and banking-like remittance practice.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: postal_money_orders now depends on banking and no longer directly depends on civic_contract_registries.
would_reject_if: A source demonstrates that the node is intentionally scoped to ancient courier payment instructions rather than modern postal money orders.
A source shows civic contract registries were the direct institutional basis for postal money orders.
A separate postal_banking node is added and becomes a better prerequisite than banking.
short_reason: Postal money orders are a postal banking/remittance product, not a civic contract registry technology.
